R
Blockchain technology is gaining popularity and is being
C used  in various  industries  [1]–[4]. This work focuses on
.
s its application to real  estate,  specifically to the rental of
c
[ properties [5]–[10]. The traditional property rental process
 
  has evolved with technological advancements, yet  there re-
1 mains room for  improvement. Opportunities start with the
v
complexity of  the property rental market, which  involves
0
not only state regulations but also considerations related to
8
1 lease types. Moreover, documentation is exchanged through
6 multiple  insecure channels,  such as e-mail  and messaging
0 applications. This violates privacy laws such as the European
.
4 General Data Protection Regulation [11]. The present practice
0 for creating rental  agreements  forces participants who are
5
unfamiliar with the process  to carry out different  types of
2
: contracts. More critically,  the different  stakeholders do not
v trust each other. This lack of trust in the property rental process
i
drives some homeowners away from the market and forces
X
r others to increase rent prices to cover potential  losses. This
a is a particularly important issue due to the housing crisis that
has intensified in recent years.
Blockchain can provide consistency and additional  trust
in the property rental process. The use of smart contracts
to enforce lease agreement conditions reduces the need for
the entities involved to trust each other and to know how to
force the agreed clauses. Permissioned ledgers allow users
to control what data they share and with who they share
it with. Furthermore,  these ledgers allow the imposition of
entry barriers for new users, supporting Know Your Customer
(KYC) processes [12],  thus enhancing security. Transactions
are facilitated by the creation of smart rental contracts, i.e., the
implementation of a rental contract as a smart contract. Smart
contracts are immutable code that  runs on the blockchain.
These smart rental contracts automatically execute contractual
agreements, this automation enhances trust and increases pro-
cess efficiency. Due to its immutable nature, information stored

1
r Real Estate Rental
2  1
Tiago Dias  Miguel Correia
iversidade de Lisboa - Lisbon, Portugal
bon, Portugal
oa.pt {joao.santos, tiago.dias}@unlockit.io
in the blockchain cannot be altered, providing a trustworthy
and unchangeable record. The blockchain creates a trusting
environment between the tenant and the landlord, mitigating
fraud opportunities.
Decentralization on blockchains is achieved through con-
sensus mechanisms. These mechanisms follow deterministic
rules to ensure that all nodes reach the same conclusion about
the state of the blockchain. For example, given the same set
of transactions and state, every node should end up with the
same final state after the process. This restricts the execution of
smart contracts to the data already stored on-chain. However,
most of  the capabilities of smart contracts are lost without
access to external data.
Blockchain oracles are services that enable smart contracts
to interact with real-world data. Many types of oracle exist
with different  characteristics and functionalities  [13]–[15].
They can be applied in many different  systems, operating
differently depending on what  they were designed for.  Im-
plementing oracles poses significant challenges as they can be
seen as potential centralized points of failure in a decentralized
environment or introduce concerns related to security and trust.
The paper proposes two oracles to make the rental process
more  trustworthy,  specifically during  the  lease period by
addressing two key challenges: delayed rent payments and
determining responsibility for property issues that arise during
the lease;  in the document,  these are referred to as Main-
tenance Issues (MIs). These problems can include scenarios
such as a broken chair, a power failure, or a broken window
due to bad weather. Using the immutability of a blockchain, it
is possible to store the details of all the issues and interactions
between the parties. To assess the benefits of these two oracles,
a blockchain-based property rental platform developed using
the Daml ledger model [16] is also presented. The platform
integrates the two oracles: Automated Rent Collection Oracle
(ARC-Oracle) to streamline rent payments; and Maintenance
Issues Oracle (MI-Oracle) to manage and document property-
related problems. The former is fully automated, but the latter
obtains its data from humans, which we call arbitrators. We
evaluate the oracles in terms of performance and security.

Daml  is a functional programming language designed to
implement smart contracts [16]. The Daml  ledger model  is
a virtual permissioned ledger  that allows workflows among
different parties. These workflows are written in Daml and
executed as smart contracts. A party can be a legal entity, a

When talking about blockchain-based rental systems, there
are a few start-up companies working in the area [19], [20],

The paper proposes two oracles, but also the platform used
to assess  them. The platform uses  the Canton Protocol,  a
permissioned blockchain,  to control  the actions each entity
can perform on  the platform and  to allow each user  to
maintain the privacy of its data. In the Canton Protocol, each
node has one or more parties connected to it. The nodes
connect  to synchronization domains (sync domains). These
domains handle authentication and data transport. In the sync
domain,  the sequencer is responsible for ordering messages
and delivering them to the participants for validation, as well
as timestamping the transactions. The mediator receives the
responses from the participants and aggregates them into a
single verdict  for  the entire confirmation request, and it  is
used to hide the participant’s entities from each other.
A. Platform and Oracles Overview
This platform supports the workflow of the creation of a
Lease Agreement between Tenants and Landlords, plus the
additional  functionalities provided by the oracles.  It  is  in
line with the privacy and authorization required for  these
workflows. The ARC-Oracle functions as a daily trigger  to
process rent payments. The payment of the rent is considered
to be itself a smart contract, called an IOU (I Owe You), that
acknowledges the existence of a debt. The MI-Oracle is used
to solve disputes when MIs occur during the lease period. A
maintenance issue (MI) is associated with a lease agreement.
It has a starting date, a duration, and a repair cost. When
resolved, it will have a percentage of responsibility attributed
to the Landlord and the Tenant. The resolution is represented
by a smart contract with the MI details and the percentage of
responsibility.
1) Participants: The users of this platform are the Tenant,
who rents  the property,  and the Landlord, who grants  the
right  to use the property in exchange for  the rent. Tenants
and Landlords are  the Users.  In addition,  there are  two
more human entities on the platform,  the Operator and the
Arbitrator. The former  is considered a source of  trust: he
initializes the ledger and is a stakeholder in the contracts that
rely on outer entities. The latter has the role of providing a
report of any MI that can occur during the lease period (see
Fig. 1).

Fig. 1. General use case diagram
Fig. 2. General system architecture
2) Lease Agreement Creation: For simplicity, on this plat-
form, the Lease Agreement (LA) creation process begins with
the Tenant submitting a Proposal. This is done through his
front-end interface, User UI, specifying the House to rent and
the Lease Terms. The Lease Terms contain the rent, beginning
date, payment dates, and the number of arbitrators to be called
when an MI occurs. The Landlord can then accept or reject the
Proposal. Before the Landlord makes a decision,  the Tenant
has the option to withdraw the Proposal.  If the Proposal  is
rejected or withdrawn, the Tenant can submit a new Proposal
with different Lease Terms. An LA Request  contract will
be generated if the Landlord accepts the Proposal. The LA
contract will be created with the information from the LA
Request once this request is validated by the Operator.
B. System Architecture
The architecture is represented in Fig. 2. It consists of an
application back-end to manage smart contracts, two UIs, one
for the users and the other for the arbitrators, and an API
to connect  the schedulers to the oracle node. Users interact
with the blockchain-based platform via a User UI, which
communicates with the Users’ node to process actions such
as managing lease agreements or  reporting MIs. The node
interacts exclusively with the MI-Module and LA-Module.
The MI-Oracle has a similar design. The Arbitrators interact
with the Arbitrator UI, which communicates with the MI-
Oracle node that  interacts exclusively with the MI-Module.
Each User and Arbitrator  is  represented as an individual
party and must  trust  the Users’ node and MI-Oracle node
respectively. The users do not need to trust other parties, the
Arbitrators are assumed to be trusted parties. Each of the nodes
can be replicated to scale the application. However, before
joining, each node must be approved by the sync domain.

3
Fig. 3. ARC-Oracle architecture
The ARC-Oracle consists of a scheduler  implemented in
the ARC-Oracle node. The node interacts only with the ARC-
Module. In the figure, TimeProvider and Lifecycler belong to
the ARC-Oracle node. However, from the perspective of the
smart contract’s back-end, each party has a different  func-
tion. The TimeProvider updates the platform date clock. The
Lifecycler initiates the rent collection workflows. Optionally,
a node instance can be configured where both TimeProvider
and Lifecycler functions are executed by the same party.
C. Automatic Rent Collection Oracle (ARC-Oracle)
The ARC-Oracle can be classified as a push-based inbound
oracle.

It does not have a data source because it  functions
purely as a trigger;  there is no need to retrieve data from
external sources. The oracle solution is composed of a set
of nodes  (ARC-Oracle nodes)  and the ARC-Module. The
architecture of the ARC-Module is shown in Fig. 3.
Two  transactions are executed daily  to collect  the  rent
payments. The first  is the DateClock smart contract update
by the TimeProvider. The DateClockUpdate smart contract
is created within this transaction. The second is to process
the date update. The Lifecycler initiates the collection of rent
in the Evolve contract using DateClockUpdate as proof of
the passing of  the day. The Evolve contract, which stores
a set of Lease Agreements (LA) on the platform,  identifies
lease agreements with overdue payments and generates an
IOU contract for each,  representing the Tenant’s debt  to the
Landlord with the value of the rent.
In the DateClock update,  the new date is set to the value
of  the  transaction timestamp.  In Daml  each transaction is
associated with two timestamps: Ledger Time and Record
Time. Ledger Time is set by the submitting participant and
the Record Time is assigned by the sync domain when the
transaction is recorded. The Record Time is acknowledged
as accurate. The model guarantees that the Record Time and
Ledger Time deviate by no more than the skew value. The
Ledger Time can be obtained by calling the getTime function
[25] The correctness of this value depends on the sync domain
and the submitting party. Tampering this value would require
collusion between these two entities.

Fig. 4. MI-Oracle Archimate diagram
The DateClockUpdate is created to prevent making the
DateClock public,  as  this would allow any user  to try to
submit a transaction to advance the clock. Although tamper-
ing with the date would still  require the agreement of  the
synchronization domain, it could expose the system to Denial
of Service attacks and block the TimeProvider from advancing
the time. The DateClockUpdate provides the current date to
the whole system. By referencing this contract  instead of
calling the getTime function, it ensures that if two transactions
that require the date occur back-to-back at midnight, they will
share the date, or only the second transaction will have the
date of  the following day. The first  transaction can never
have the date after  the second transaction. This cannot be
assured when calling the getTime function, since there is a
skew value between the record time and the ledger time [25].
This mitigates races when two transactions happen back-to-
back at midnight.
D. Maintenance Issues Oracle (MI-Oracle)
The MI-Oracle is a Human Oracle. It can be classified as
a pull-based inbound oracle. The MI-Oracle architecture is
simple since it retrieves data from a human data source. The
Arbitrator UI manipulates  three smart contracts:  Invitation,
MI Report, and Poll. As shown in Fig. 4, the UI is used by
the Arbitrator. For each MI, one of the Arbitrators will be a
Visitor. Its role is to physically visit the property to evaluate the
reported issue and to create a poll. This poll allows the rest of
the Arbitrators to vote on the level of responsibility assigned
to each stakeholder. There is no specific process for selecting
the Visitor. However, its identity is recorded on-chain.
A MI Report can be created by the tenant or the landlord
if any property problem arises during the lease period. The
report contains the starting date and the description of  the
issue. In the platform there is the option for MI issues to be
resolved by mediation;  this is out of  the scope of  the MI-
Oracle. The resolution by mediation is a more convenient
option for both the Tenant and the Landlord since it does
not bear the additional costs of allocating Arbitrators in this
process,  smart contracts are used as  the mediators for  the

4
Fig. 5.  BPMN diagram of the Arbitrators invitation process
Fig. 6.  Arbitrators polling process
resolution. The resolution by mediation is not part of  the
formal arbitration process;  rather,  it serves as an additional
step in the MI resolution process or as an alternative to it.
The Arbitration process is more complex and involves two
phases, the invitation and the polling. Triggering this process
is a unilateral decision by the Tenant and Landlord, but both
parties can perform the same actions throughout the procedure.
The invitation process is shown in the diagram of Fig. 5.
The invitation is sent to all available arbitrators. This invitation
remains active until  the required number of arbitrators  is
reached. It is important to state that only legitimate Arbitrator
entities are invited. Having an off-chain list of the legitimate
Arbitrator entities on the user client  is not a safe approach
since it could easily be tampered with and there would be no
point  in using a DLT.

To address this, a publicly accessible
smart contract AvailableArbitrators maintains a list of all
available Arbitrator parties. Only the Operator entity can add
new Arbitrators to the smart contract list.
The polling process shown in Fig. 6 is pretty straightfor-
ward. After the Visitor evaluates the issue in the property, it
creates the Poll smart contract that includes all the relevant
details for the other Arbitrators to provide an accurate vote.
A decision on the N arbitrators to attribute to an MI will be
required to assess a trade-off between cost and performance. A.

This section provides a description of the implementation of
a prototype to demonstrate the use of oracles. The Daml ledger
guarantees authorization and privacy, i.e., that all actions that
affect a party were previously authorized by him and that if a
party is not involved in an action it does not know it happened.

5
A. Back-end Implementation
The back-end is implemented using the Daml language, and
to fully understand the implementation decisions is important
to understand the Daml  ledger model.  In the Daml  ledger
model it is not possible to prevent contracts from being created
directly, which means that any party can create any contract.
In Daml, contracts are created using templates. Contracts
contain data,  referred to as the create arguments,  these ar-
guments have a name and a  type. Unlike Java classes,  a
contract cannot change its create arguments, a new contract
must be created. We refer to the create arguments as attributes
because most time we can think about a contract as a class.
Each contract must have one or more signatories,  parties
whose authority is required to create the contract or archive it.
Additionally, a contract can have observers, parties who can
see the contract and all actions performed on it. When a new
instance of a contract is created, a contract Id is attributed to
it.
If we think about  the contract  templates as classes and
contracts as objects, choices would be the methods. They are
predefined actions that can be exercised in the contract. They
allow the signatories to delegate the right to perform a data
transformation in a contract. Daml choices have controllers,
which are the parties that have the right to exercise them.
1) Lease Agreement Creation: The implementation of a
Lease Agreement Proposal  follows a Propose and Accept
Pattern to ensure both parties sign the Lease Terms proposed.
It functions as follows, the Tenant creates a Proposal contract
that includes the LeaseTerms,  the House they intend to rent,
and the Operator party. Within this contract, the Landlord can
exercise the Decline choice and the Tenant can exercise the
Withdraw choice; both options will archive the contract. The
Landlord can also exercise the Accept choice, which archives
the contract and creates a LACreationRequest contract.  In
the LACreationRequest contract  is required the Operator’s
permission to create the LeaseAgreement contract.
2) ARC-Oracle:  The automatic rent  collection back-end
was designed with scalability in mind while also addressing
potential  threats from malicious actors. This process can be
divided into two parts: updating the DateClock contract and
lifecycling the rent payments. In this context, the term lifecycle
means  to verify  in all  lease agreements  if  there are due
payments and, if so, to process them.
In the smart contracts implementation, the TimeProvider and
Lifecycler considered different parties.
The DateClock contract has the following attributes. The
providers, a set of parties that are authorized to advance the
date in the contract. This is needed for when there is more
than one node communicating with the same ARC-Module, to
increase fault tolerance. The creator, which is the last party
to update the date. The waitingAccept that  is used to add
providers to the contract. This is done by the Operator.
To update  the date,  one of  the providers exercises  the
Advance choice in the contract. This choice calls the getTime
function that returns the value of the transaction timestamp.
The current date is obtained from this value and an offset is
calculated from this date value and the date on the DateClock
contract. If this offset is larger than 0 then a new DateClock

and DateClockUpdate are created,  and  the old ones  are
archived. .
The Evolve contract contains a set of laKeys. These are
the keys to every lease agreement associated with the Evolve
contract. The signatory of this contract is the Operator.
There are two choices in this contract. The AddLA choice
adds  a  laKey  to  the  lakeys  set  in  the  Evolve  con-
tract.  It  can only be exercised by the Operator. And the
ProcessEvent choice,  is exercised by either  the Lifecycler.
This choice  iterates  through all  laKeys and exercises  the
ProcessPayment choice on each LeaseAgereement contract
associated with the key.

The ProcessPayment choice requires
the DateClockUpdate contract as proof of the current date and
checks which payment dates are earlier than the date specified
in the DateClockUpdate, for these dates it creates an IOU with
the Landlord as owner and the Tenant as debtor.
3) MI-Oracle: The MI process is triggered by calling the
CreateMI choice in the LeaseAgreement contract with the fol-
lowing parameters: house, description, and startingDate.
This choice creates the MIReport contract below.
MIReport
tenant : Party
landlord.: Party
laKey : LAkey
miDetails: MIdetails
arbitrators : Parties
activeInvitation : Bool
signatory tenant, landlord
observer arbitrators
The MIReport is initialized with the arbitrators attribute
as an empty set.
The  resolution  by  mediation  is  triggered  by  the
SubmitAssessment  choice which  requires  the  following
parameters:  the  creator  (either  the Tenant  or  Landlord),
the assessment details, which includes the responsibility of
each party and the cost of  repairs, and the contract  Id of
the associated MiReport contract. This contract  Id is used
to fetch the real number of arbitrators agreed in the lease
terms. This choice creates  the MediationAssessment with
the creator as a signatory and the counterpart as an observer.
To accept  this assessment,  the counterpart must  sign this
contract by exercising the AcceptAssessment choice in the
MediationAssessment  contract.  This  choice  archives  the
contract  and creates  the MIresultMediation contract.  To
reject  the assessment,  the counterpart exercises  the Reject
choice that archives the contract.
In the resolution by arbitration the first step is to assign
Arbitrators to the MIReport contract. Only legitimate Arbi-
trator entities must be  invited. This  is achieved with the
AvailableArbitrators and AvailableArbitratorsRequest
contracts below.
AvailableArbitrators
operator : Party
arbitrators : Parties
observers : Parties
signatory operator
observer observers
AvailableArbitratorsRequest
public : Party

6
requester : Party
signatory requester
observer public
To create the InviteArbitrators contract where the Arbi-
trators can accept  the invitation. One of the parties, Tenant
or Landlord exercises the InvokeArbitrators choice in the
LeaseAgreement contract. With an AvailableArbitrators
contract Id and MIReport contract Id as parameters.
It  is impractical  to include all platform users in the ob-
server  set  of  the AvailableArbitrators attribute. More-
over, some of these users may never interact with this con-
tract. The  following describes  a more  scalable approach.
The observers attribute of  the AvailableArbitrators  is
initialized with  the public party. All  users can read and
act  in  the name of  this party. However,  this party can-
not  be  an observer  on  the  LeaseAgreement  for  privacy
reasons. Therefore, we have  to create an  instance of  the
AvailableArbitrators contract where only the party that
will  invite the Arbitrators is an observer. For  this, we use
AvailableArbitratorsRequest that any party can create. By
providing the AvailableArbitratorsRequest contract Id as a
parameter, anyone can exercise the AddObserver choice. This
creates a new instance of the AvailableArbitrators contract
with the requester as an observer.
The InvokeArbitrators choice starts by verifying  that
there is no Invitation Process currently active,  through the
activeInvitation flag.  If not,  it  sets  it  to true. Then it
fetches the attributes of the MIReport contract and archives
it. The instance of the AvailableArbitrators contract cre-
ated previously is  fetched to retrieve  the arbitrators at-
tribute and then archived. The Operator  is the only signa-
tory of  the AvailableArbitrators contract and,  therefore,
is  the only entity with the right  to archive  it. To assure
the signatory of  the AvailableArbitrators contract  is the
real Operator party, we confirm that  the party is different
from the Tenant and Landlord. Note that,  if another party
had created the AvailableArbitrators,  impersonating the
Operator,  the InvokeArbitrators choice would have failed
in the MIReport fetch above. This is because this entity would
not be a signatory in the LeaseAgreement contract. For this
reason,  the InvokeArbitrators choice must be exercised in
the LeaseAgreement contract, where the Operator, Tenant,
and Landlord are signatories. After  these verifications,  the
InviteArbitrators contract associated with the MIReport is
created.
In  the InviteArbitrators contract,  all Arbitrators can
exercise the Accept choice. This adds  them to the list of
confirmed Arbitrators. When the list is full, that is, when its
size is equal to the number of arbitrators agreed on in the lease
terms. Either the Tenant or Landlord finalizes the invitation by
exercising the ConfirmAttribution choice.

This choice gets
the confirmed parties from the InviteArbitrators contract to
ensure that the Arbitrators are set from an InviteArbitrators
contract. It then archives the InviteArbitrators and assigns
the confirmed Arbitrators to the MIReport.
In the Polling Process,  the Visitor, will visit  the property
and create the Poll contract represented below, by exercising

the CreatePoll choice in the MIReport contract. This choice
can only be exercised by the Arbitrators party.
Poll
tenant : Party
landlord.: Party
miDetails : MIdetails
visitor : Party
visitDetails : Text
assessmentDate : Date
reparationDate : Date
cost: Int
voters : Parties
alreadyVoted : Parties
votes : [Responsability]
signatory alreadyVoted, tenant, landlord
observer  voters
This contract  is created with the alreadyVoted attribute
containing only the Visitor. Both the Tenant and Landlord must
be signatory to this contract. The voters attribute contains
all Arbitrators  that will vote (the same that confirmed the
invitation). A vote is submitted by exercising the Vote choice,
this archives  the contract and creates a new instance with
the voter in the alreadyVoted list and its vote in the votes
list. The choice verifies that the voter is not already on this
list, preventing duplicate votes. The members of this list are
signatories of the contract, so they can not deny the vote. When
all votes are completed, any entity can finalize the poll by
exercising the FinalizeVotation choice. This choice archives
the Poll contract and creates a MIresultArbitration contract
with the results of the poll, that is, the mean of all votes. This
choice asserts the voters set is equal to the alreadyVoted set.
In the Arbitrator UI, this choice is executed automatically by
the last Arbitrator submitting the vote.
B. Back-end Interaction Layer
This  layer  consists of  the User UI, Arbitrator UI,  and
the ARC-Oracle API. The  interaction with  the  ledger  is
enabled by the Daml Java Bindings. The User and Arbitrator
interact with the system through a command-line interface.
The User UI and Arbitrator UI have the same structure: A
LedgerCommunication class to interact with the ledger and
an IO class  to  interact with  the stdout. The ARC-Oracle
API has a LedgerCommunication class. A scheduler could be
implemented using the Java ScheduledExecutorService class
with a 24-hour period. However, to simplify development and
testing, we opted to implement the trigger as input from the
stdin.
1) Ledger Communication:  In the LedgerCommunication
class the necessary parameters to communicate with the ledger
are: the ledger’s IP address, the port, and the party Ids. It has an
in-memory contract store that provides a live view of all active
contracts it  interacts with that maps contract Ids to external
Ids. The contract  store  is updated to a recent  state using
the Active Contract Service from the Daml  Java Bindings
library. The LedgerCommunication class has a probe function
using the Transaction Service that continuously updates the
contract store in response to all  ledger events that create or
archive contracts. It is important to remember that contracts
can only be seen, therefore, stored, when the party has a stake

7
in the contract. The probe function creates a new thread to
update the contract  store in the background. This function
uses the Flowable class of  the RxJava library to deal with
the backpressure of  the events  received from the  ledger.
A ConcurrentHashMap is used to map the external  Ids  to
each Contract and a HashBiMap wrapped around a thread-safe
BiMap. For the same reason, it is used AtomicLong to get and
update the external Ids. The contracts stored in each interface
are as follows:
User UI:  (i) LeaseAgreement: Live view.  (ii) MIReport:
Live view. (iii) InviteArbitrators: Not stored, probed to check
if agreed number of arbitrators reached.  (iv) AvailableArbi-
tratorsRequest: Not stored, created by the user, argument of
AddObserver.  (v) AvailableArbitrators: Live view, only one
contract in the ledger so stored in most recent contract instance
(fetched with public party). (vi) IOU: Not stored, probed when
a new instance is created, notification displayed.
Arbitrator UI:  (i)  InviteArbitrators: Live view but only
incomplete invitations where the party has not yet confirmed
are stored. (ii) MIReport: Live view. (iii) Poll: Live view.
ARC-Oracle API:  (i) DateClock: Live view,  only one
contract visible per party in the ledger, so only most recent
instance stored.  (ii) Evolve: Same as DateClock.  (iii) Date-
ClockUpdateEvent: No stored, obtained from return value of
Advance.
The commands that are submitted to the ledger are encoded
in an instance of the Update class. They are submitted to the
ledger by the Command Submission Service from the Ledger
API Services. The commands are submitted synchronously.
2) UI Interaction:  In the User UI, there are three naviga-
tion menus: Main, Lease Agreements, and MIs menus.

The
navigation options in each one of the menus are:
Main Menu:  (i) Lease Agreements List: Displays the lease
agreements the user is associated with.  (ii) Make Proposal:
For the user to create a proposal. (iii) Proposals List: Lists
the proposals waiting confirmation. (iv) Display all contracts:
Displays the in-memory contract store (for debugging).
Lease Agreements Menu:  (i) MIs List: Lists the MIs asso-
ciated with the selected lease agreement. (ii) Create Mainte-
nance Issue: For the user to create a new MI associated with
the selected lease agreement. (iii) Back to main menu: Ditto.
Maintenance Issues Menu:  (i) Mediation Resolution: For
the user to submit an assessment to resolve the issue. (ii) Me-
diation Proposals: Lists the mediation proposals for this MI.
(iii) Arbitration Resolution: Triggers the Arbitration resolution
process by calling  the invokeArbitrators method  in  the
LedgerCommunication instance.
In the Arbitrator UI, there is one Main Menu with three nav-
igation options, these are: (i) List invitations to MIs Reports:
Lists all invitations; the Arbitrator can then select an invitation
and accept or reject. (ii) List Assigned MIs Reports: Lists all
MIReport contracts that the Arbitrator is associated with. The
Arbitrator can then select  the MI to create a poll. Then he
provides the details of the visit, the cost of the repair, the date
of the assessment, the date of the repair, and its vote. (iii) List
Polls: Lists all polls to which the Arbitrator is assigned. He
can then select the poll for which he wants to vote. Displays.

The evaluation criteria are different  for each oracle. The
latency of the ARC-Oracle will be assessed and the MI-Oracle
will be compared with the current approach to solving disputes
between tenants and landlords. The whole platform will be
assessed in terms of security, mainly integrity and privacy.
A. ARC-Oracle Evaluation
The ARC-Oracle is evaluated against the latency of com-
pleting its  two transactions, advancing the DateClock con-
tract and verifying and processing the due payments. The
latency value is calculated as a function of  the number of
LeaseAgreement contracts associated with the Evolve contract.
It was tested with a single ARC-Oracle node running in a
Canton ledger with a single node and a single synchronization
domain, in a MacBook with a 1,4 GHz Quad-Core Intel Core
i5 processor and 16GB of RAM. Latency was measured with
no due payments, half due payments and all due payments.
The values are represented in Fig. 7.
From the analysis, the number of due payments in the lease
agreement smart contracts associated with the Evolve smart
contract has an impact on latency. According to what is ex-
pected, a higher number of due payments increases the latency
of the request. Since rent payments are typically scheduled on
the 25th of each month, the red and blue lines provide more
descriptive results. Optimizing just for these peak times,  the
25th of each month, might allow better resource usage. There
is no point in evaluating the throughput as there is always one
transaction per day.
B. MI-Oracle Evaluation
The MI-Oracle  is designed  to be an alternative  to  the
traditional  litigation process for resolving disputes between
tenants and landlords. To evaluate this process, we compare
the resources consumed in the current process with those
consumed when using the MI-Oracle.

8
To compare the duration of a dispute with the traditional
litigation process and our solution, we provide an overview of
the traditional  litigation process. This process begins with a
civil action, the legal process through which an entity initiates
a lawsuit against another party to resolve a civil dispute. A
civil execution follows a civil action if the judgment or order
of  the civil action is not voluntarily complied with by the
losing party. In Lisbon, the average duration of a civil action
in 2022 was 12 months and of a civil execution was 56 months
[26]. These statistics do not include the time to hire a lawyer.
Our solution does not  include a process to solve a civil
execution,  as  this would  require granting  the Arbitrators
judicial power, so the reference used is the average duration
of a civil  action, 12 months.  In Lisbon,  there are around
120000 rental agreements per year [27]. Using the MI-Oracle
process during the lease period reduces the waiting time for
the problem solution from months to days. The reason is that
the duration depends on the availability of the Arbitrator to
visit the property, and the Tenant and Landlord. The duration
of traditional litigation depends on factors such as the time it
takes to find a lawyer,  the availability of both parties,  their
lawyers and a jury, the court’s schedule, among others.
The traditional process requires a lawyer for consultation
and representation of each party, as well as a jury. In the MI-
Oracle process,  it  is necessary to allocate a Visitor plus the
defined number of Arbitrators in the lease agreement. If the
agreed number of Arbitrators is small, the traditional litigation
process will use more human resources than our solution.
C. Platform Security
The platform is created to provide a proposal service and
a lease agreement management service. This security analysis
considers a threat any breach in confidentiality or unauthorized
access  that  compromises  the protections  enforced by  the
smart contract implementation. We assume that a user party,
Artbitrator party, and Operator party cannot be impersonated,
which means that any action attributed to the party was carried
out by the individual associated with that party.
We used the Daml  testing tools, mainly Daml Script,  for
this evaluation. Daml Script  is a method for  testing Daml
models and receiving feedback in Daml Studio, which is an
IDE designed specifically to work with Daml. Our platform
is  in agreement with the security requirements  in the cre-
ation of the following contracts: LeaseAgreement, MIReport,
MIReportMediation, Poll, MIReportResults. As an example,
we show that a lease agreement cannot be created without the
consent of both the Tenant and Landlord. The LeaseAgreement
contract cannot be created directly from its template without
the consent of the Tenant, Landlord and Operator, because
they are all signators. The only alternative way to create a
LeaseAgreement contract is for the Operator to exercise the
Approve choice in the LACreationRequest contract,  in this
contract both the Tenant and Landlord are signatories.

There is
only one way to create this contract, which is for the Landlord
to exercise the Accept choice in the Proposal contract created
by the Tenant. Before accepting,  the Landlord can confirm
the lease terms, the house, and the Operator party. To evaluate.

This paper introduced two oracles for blockchain-supported
property rental, one for automating payments and another for
supporting MI management. The paper also presents a property
rental platform that  is used to assess the two oracles. This
work focused first on finding the time-based trigger solution
that best aligned with the needs of the rental platform. It relies
on obtaining the transaction timestamp and converting it into
a date value. The disputes in a property rental environment
have characteristics that make it a more challenging problem to
address: the number of issues that can appear in a property, the
property being a private and static structure, and the subjective
aspect of the assessment. These characteristics require us to
use humans to perform the assessment,  implying an oracle
with UI.
